caveats: a: The sources describe occupations and the broad library labor market, not the small eligible commercial subset of 519210.
a: No reliable eligible-firm occupation mix or wage weights were found.
a: The frozen labor input comes from ancestor NAICS 519 and includes activities unlike libraries and archives, so it may materially misstate labor intensity.
rho: BTOS is a business survey and is poorly matched to the public and nonprofit institutions common in this industry.
rho: Reported AI use does not measure the share of exposed work converted in production.
e: The provided eligible firm-count input is missing, so the absolute opportunity cannot be quantified from the frozen dataset.
e: No source directly measures the share of 519210 establishments that are independent commercial firms in the EBITDA band.
e: Some private service providers performing archive work may be classified in document management, consulting, digitization, or records storage instead.
s5: This is bounded judgment without observed industry deal flow.
s5: The missing eligible-firm count makes even a small probability difficult to interpret in absolute terms.
s5: Internal reorganizations and changes in public or nonprofit governance are excluded.
q: No direct evidence on retained automation benefits was found.
q: Economics differ fundamentally among commercial contractors, membership organizations, nonprofits, and publicly funded institutions.
d5: The BLS outlook is for librarians rather than all 519210 service quantity and spans ten years rather than five.
d5: Employment can diverge from demand because of productivity, budgets, volunteers, and changes in service mix.
o: Task descriptions establish responsibility but do not quantify the share of service volume attached to each duty.
o: Operator need varies sharply by physical versus digital collections and by legal, cultural, privacy, and authenticity requirements.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Eligible operators may earn memberships or subscriptions, recurring institutional contracts, and repeat research, retrieval, digitization, preservation, or collection-management fees. The broader industry also contains appropriation-, grant-, and donation-funded organizations that frequently do not fit the default firm lens.
lens.excluded_activities: Government agencies; public, school, university, museum, corporate, legal, medical, or other captive internal collections; entities without an external recurring or repeat service model; shells; non-control financing vehicles; and businesses classified as stock media, web search portals, or other information services.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market independent operators providing recurring or repeat outsourced library, archive, collection-access, research, retrieval, cataloging, digitization, preservation, and records-access services to external customers or members.

a.bridge: BLS and O*NET describe librarian and archivist duties rather than measuring the wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task share of eligible 519210 firms. The task lists are classified into exposure for search and retrieval, cataloging and metadata, routine patron communication, program administration, digitization support, and drafting, then discounted for physical handling, preservation, provenance, authentication, instruction, policy, and accountable judgment. This can overstate exposure because many digital tasks already use databases and self-service systems, or understate it by omitting administrative and support occupations and newer multimodal AI capabilities.
caveats: The sources describe occupations and the broad library labor market, not the small eligible commercial subset of 519210.
No reliable eligible-firm occupation mix or wage weights were found.
The frozen labor input comes from ancestor NAICS 519 and includes activities unlike libraries and archives, so it may materially misstate labor intensity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-task study of staff at independent contract libraries, archives, and collection-service operators, identifying existing automation and separating digital from physical collections, would materially change this value.
a.rationale: Librarian and archivist work includes database search, reference support, catalog and circulation records, descriptions, access systems, patron-request analysis, instructional materials, and program administration, all of which contain AI-exposed tasks. Collection selection, physical preservation, provenance and authenticity judgments, rights and access policy, community instruction, and sensitive patron interactions limit substitution.

d5.bridge: BLS projects librarian employment to grow 2% from 2024 to 2034 and reports that traditional borrowing is declining while demand for services and programs continues. Occupation employment and qualitative outlook are converted to a five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand ratio for the current library and archive service basket. This may understate service demand if productivity or digital access expands quantity with fewer workers, or overstate it if public budgets and establishment closures reduce services even while replacement hiring persists.
caveats: The BLS outlook is for librarians rather than all 519210 service quantity and spans ten years rather than five.
Employment can diverge from demand because of productivity, budgets, volunteers, and changes in service mix.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year cohorts of visits, searches, retrievals, digitized items, research requests, archival access, preservation volume, memberships, and constant-quality service revenue for 519210 establishments would materially change this value.
d5.rationale: Traditional lending faces pressure, but research assistance, digital access, preservation, archival stewardship, community programs, and information-literacy needs support broadly stable demand. The modest employment outlook and mixed service trends do not justify a strong directional forecast.

caveats: The provided eligible firm-count input is missing, so the absolute opportunity cannot be quantified from the frozen dataset.
No source directly measures the share of 519210 establishments that are independent commercial firms in the EBITDA band.
Some private service providers performing archive work may be classified in document management, consulting, digitization, or records storage instead.
e.change_evidence: A manually classified Census Business Register or commercial-company universe for 519210 with legal form, ownership, customers, recurring revenue, normalized EBITDA, and captive or public status would materially change this value.
e.rationale: Most recognizable libraries and archives are public, school, university, museum, nonprofit, or captive institutional operations rather than independent firms supplying a recurring outsourced service with normalized EBITDA in the target band. A small subset of private special-library, archival, research, digitization, preservation, and collection-service operators may qualify.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 519 ($54,442,667,789) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 519 ($326,617,849,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 519. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1728 (margins.json[51]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Information Services EBITDA/Sales 17.28% (conservative sector default)); revenue/employee $835,402 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 519); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

o.bridge: The NAICS definition and BLS/O*NET task descriptions identify service components that require collection custody, preservation, access policy, provenance, authentication, selection, patron instruction, and program accountability, rather than directly measuring operator-required year-five quantity. Those duties are mapped to an operator-required share after allowing search, checkout, basic retrieval, transcription, metadata drafting, and routine communication to become self-service or software-supplied. The result can be too high for born-digital standardized collections or too low for rare, physical, rights-restricted, or sensitive materials.
caveats: Task descriptions establish responsibility but do not quantify the share of service volume attached to each duty.
Operator need varies sharply by physical versus digital collections and by legal, cultural, privacy, and authenticity requirements.
o.change_evidence: Service-level data separating fully self-served digital transactions from staff-mediated retrieval, rights review, preservation, authentication, instruction, and physical access across eligible operators would materially change this value.
o.rationale: Discovery and routine access can become much more self-service, but accountable custody, preservation, provenance, authentication, rights and access decisions, collection development, instruction, and physical handling remain institutionally operated. AI changes the interface and labor mix more readily than it eliminates the responsible operator.

caveats: No direct evidence on retained automation benefits was found.
Economics differ fundamentally among commercial contractors, membership organizations, nonprofits, and publicly funded institutions.
q.change_evidence: Contract and budget cohorts for private operators showing fee structure, competitive rebids, renewal repricing, service-level changes, reinvestment, and margin changes following automation would materially change this value.
q.rationale: Private subscription and fixed-fee archive-service contracts can retain some implemented savings, but many institutions are budget-constrained, grant- or appropriation-funded, cost-sensitive, or evaluated on service expansion rather than distributable margin. Renewals, competitive bids, customer demands for lower fees, and reinvestment in access, digitization, preservation, and programming reduce retention.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures current AI use among US employer businesses, including a high-level Information sector, not implementation of exposed library and archive tasks over five years. Broad adoption is projected forward and discounted for public-sector procurement, privacy, copyright, provenance, hallucination risk, small budgets, legacy collection systems, and digitization prerequisites. The result may be too high because many libraries are government or nonprofit entities outside BTOS, or too low for commercial digital-archive specialists with machine-readable collections.
caveats: BTOS is a business survey and is poorly matched to the public and nonprofit institutions common in this industry.
Reported AI use does not measure the share of exposed work converted in production.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of 519210 operators reporting production AI deployments, digital-collection readiness, procurement and rights approvals, error-review burdens, and realized staff-hour effects would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: AI tools should diffuse into retrieval assistance, metadata enrichment, transcription, summarization, patron communications, and administration, but source fidelity and accountability are central to archival work. Public and nonprofit governance, uneven digitization, limited budgets, copyright and privacy constraints, and the need for human review slow operational implementation.

caveats: This is bounded judgment without observed industry deal flow.
The missing eligible-firm count makes even a small probability difficult to interpret in absolute terms.
Internal reorganizations and changes in public or nonprofit governance are excluded.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction search in PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, and specialist broker records, matched to a verified denominator of eligible private 519210 firms and supplemented by owner-age interviews, would materially change this value.
s5.rationale: Government, educational, nonprofit, and captive institutions generally undergo budget or governance changes rather than qualifying control transfers. The small independent commercial subset can be sold, but no industry transaction denominator, succession evidence, or observed deal flow was found.

principal_driver: AI-exposed search and metadata workflows coexist with durable, accountable stewardship of collections.
principal_weakness: Most industry activity does not belong to transferable independent firms, and the eligible firm-count input is missing.

sources.supports: Industry definition, collection and access activities, and classification boundaries used for the lens and operator-need assessment.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual: 519210 Libraries and Archives

sources.supports: Librarian duties, employer distribution, 2024 employment, projected 2024-2034 employment change, openings, and qualitative service-demand outlook.

sources.supports: Detailed librarian tasks involving search, cataloging, circulation, patron assistance, instruction, programs, policy, budgets, and collection development.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Librarians and Media Collections Specialists

sources.supports: Archivist tasks involving classification, descriptions, databases, access policy, preservation, provenance, authentication, appraisal, and outreach.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Archivists Tasks

sources.supports: Current AI-use prevalence among US employer businesses and the broad Information sector, used as an implementation proxy.
sources.title: Census Bureau: Nearly 1 in 5 U.S. Businesses Use Artificial Intelligence

title: Libraries and Archives
